coredata.dc:title: Multifunction of epoxy resin with biomass carbon dot as synergetic flame retardant for outstanding smoke suppression and toxic attenuation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Peixiao
Zhang, Hongjian
Li, Xiaoli
Leng, Yang
Li, Bin
Wang, Zhao
Xu, Miaojun
coredata.dc:description: With the rapid progress of electronics and 5 G technology, high flame retardancy and low toxicity epoxy resin have been crucial. Hence, the bio-carbon dot (CD) doped with N, P, and Mo elements was prepared by hydrothermal method using corn straw as the biomass carbon source, ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source, and phosphomolybdic acid as the phosphorus and molybdenum source. As a P-containing compound, ADP complexed with CD containing the transition metal element Mo on the surface to obtain complexes (ADP/CD). The flame retardant epoxy resin composites (EP/ADP/CD) were further prepared by ADP/CD as a flame retardant and 4,4′-diaminodiphenylmethane (DDM) as a curing substance. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) were used to analyze the shape and chemical structure of CD. The experimental results indicate that when the additive amount of ADP/CD was 4 % (ADP: CD=3.5:0.5), the limiting oxygen index (LOI) of the EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD was 37.5 % (41.5 % higher than pure EP) and reached the UL94 V-0 rate. Cone calorimeter testing revealed a 51.4 %, 39.1 %, 19.6 %, and 70.4 % decrease in the peak heat release rate (pHRR), total heat release (THR), carbon monoxide production (COP), and carbon dioxide production (CO2P) of EP/ADP/CD. respectively, then with EP. Furthermore, the mechanical properties of EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD were discussed. Therefore, this work designs a flame-retardant carbon dot, providing a new scheme for creating a multifunctional epoxy resin with flame retardancy, smoke reduction, and toxicity reduction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bio-carbon dot
Aluminum diethylphosphonate
Epoxy resin
Flame retardancy

DMA analysis of EP and EP composites
Flame-retardant mechanism of EP and EP composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process of CD and EP composites.

(a) TEM images, (b) particle size distribution curve, (c) HR-TEM images, and (d) XRD pattern, (e) PL spectra of CD. (f) UV–vis spectra and the photographs of CD solution under sunlight and a UV lamp (365 nm), respectively.
(a) FT-IR spectra of CD. The (b) XPS, (c) C1s, (d) N1s, (e) O1s, and (f) Mo3d spectra of CD.
(a)TG curves of EP composites under nitrogen atmosphere. (b) DTG curves of EP composites under nitrogen atmosphere.
The vertical burning test for EP and EP composites.
LOI curves of EP and EP composites.
(a) THR, (b) HRR, (c) COP, and (d) CO2P curves for EP and EP composites.
Comparison of flame retardant performance between the epoxy resin formulations stated in the literature and this work (Cui et al., 2022; Jiang et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2021, 2023a, 2023b).
(a) Storage modulus (b)tan δ and (c) Loss modules curves for EP.
The fracture surface of (a)EP (b)EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD (c)Mechanical properties of the pure EP and EP composites
Digital and SEM Photograph of the char residue of EP (a) and EP/4ADP (b) and EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD (c) after CCT.
EDS spectra of the char residue of (a) EP and (b) EP/ADP/CD after CCT.
3D TG-IR spectra of pyrolysis products for (a) EP (b) EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD.
FTIR spectra of gaseous pyrolysis products for (a) pure EP, (b) EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD at different decomposition temperatures.
The absorbance of pyrolysis products for EP, EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD versus time: (a) hydrocarbons, (b) aromatic compounds, (c) carbonyl compounds, and (d) CO.
The Gram-Schmidt curves of pure EP and EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD nanocomposite.
Possible lame retardancy mechanism of EP composites.
The formulation of EP and EP composites.
TG and DTG data of EP and EP composites in N2 atmosphere.
Data of UL-94 vertical burning test.
CCT data of the EP and EP composites.

head.ce:title: Multifunction of epoxy resin with biomass carbon dot as synergetic flame retardant for outstanding smoke suppression and toxic attenuation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Heilongjiang Key Laboratory of Molecular Design and Preparation of Flame Retarded Materials, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Heilongjiang Key Laboratory of Molecular Design and Preparation of Flame Retarded Materials, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Heilongjiang Key Laboratory of Molecular Design and Preparation of Flame Retarded Materials, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, P China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Harbin Power Supply Co, Harbin, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Harbin Power Supply Co, Harbin, P R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the rapid progress of electronics and 5 G technology, high flame retardancy and low toxicity epoxy resin have been crucial. Hence, the bio-carbon dot (CD) doped with N, P, and Mo elements was prepared by hydrothermal method using corn straw as the biomass carbon source, ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source, and phosphomolybdic acid as the phosphorus and molybdenum source. As a P-containing compound, ADP complexed with CD containing the transition metal element Mo on the surface to obtain complexes (ADP/CD). The flame retardant epoxy resin composites (EP/ADP/CD) were further prepared by ADP/CD as a flame retardant and 4,4′-diaminodiphenylmethane (DDM) as a curing substance. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) were used to analyze the shape and chemical structure of CD. The experimental results indicate that when the additive amount of ADP/CD was 4 % (ADP: CD=3.5:0.5), the limiting oxygen index (LOI) of the EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD was 37.5 % (41.5 % higher than pure EP) and reached the UL94 V-0 rate. Cone calorimeter testing revealed a 51.4 %, 39.1 %, 19.6 %, and 70.4 % decrease in the peak heat release rate (pHRR), total heat release (THR), carbon monoxide production (COP), and carbon dioxide production (CO2P) of EP/ADP/CD. respectively, then with EP. Furthermore, the mechanical properties of EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD were discussed. Therefore, this work designs a flame-retardant carbon dot, providing a new scheme for creating a multifunctional epoxy resin with flame retardancy, smoke reduction, and toxicity reduction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most prominent thermosetting resins, epoxy resin (EP) has long been utilized in electronics and aerospace components (Leng et al., 2024) due to its exceptional mechanical qualities and adhesive strength. Unfortunately, the LOI value of EP was only 19 %, which made it prone to combustion (Huang et al., 2023). Meanwhile, some materials also emit thick smoke and toxic gases when burnt, posing a significant threat to human life, health, and property safety. Therefore, it is imperative to improve the flame retardancy of epoxy resin, reduce smoke toxicity, and minimize carbon emissions to mitigate the adverse impact on the environment and human life.

At present, flame-retardant epoxy resins were mainly categorized into intrinsic flame-retardant (Cheng et al., 2023; Shao et al., 2019) and additive flame-retardant (Peng et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2023). In the actual production process, due to the complex introduction process and relatively strict preparation conditions of intrinsic flame retardants, the application and research of intrinsic flame retardants were greatly limited. The commonly used flame retardants in epoxy resin were mainly categorized into nitrogen-based flame retardants (Zhu et al., 2020), phosphorus-based flame retardants (Liu et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022), silicone-based flame retardants (Liu et al., 2023), and carbon-based flame retardants. Phosphorus-based flame retardants had been widely studied due to their low addition and environmentally friendly characteristics (Song et al., 2023). As an organophosphorus-based flame retardant, aluminum diethylphosphonate (ADP), increased the LOI value and reached the V-0 rate of the epoxy composites when incorporated into EP (Liu et al., 2012). In order to further reduce the number of additions and to give versatility to the composites, synergistic flame retardancy with carbon-based materials was chosen. ADP has excellent qualities including resistance to hydrolysis and great heat stability. Gu et al. (Gu et al., 2020) created a flame retardant nanocomposite by homogeneously dispersing 1 wt% of the fabricated multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT)-10-(2,5-dihydroxyphenyl)-9,10-dihydro-9-oxa-10-phosphaphenanthrene-10-oxide(ODOPB) with 6.18 wt% ADP in epoxy resin. The sample met UL-94 V-0 rating requirements, and the pHRR dropped by 27.9 %. However, an excessive amount of additive can negatively impact the mechanical and electrical properties of the epoxy resin. Consequently, the development of effective halogen-free flame retardant epoxy resin with a high flame retardant element content, minimal additive amount, and no environmental contamination is the direction of future epoxy resin direction.

The addition of carbon-based nanomaterials such as C60(Wang et al., 2018), carbon nanotubes (Leng et al., 2020; Rejab et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2022), carbon fibers (Shi et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2016), graphene (Li et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021), carbon black (Shih and Jeng, 2002; Yan et al., 2019), expandable graphite (Yang et al., 2016; Yuan et al., 2023) and other carbon-based nanomaterials (Leng et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018), provide epoxy resins with excellent properties, including high flame retardancy, low smoke, and low toxicity. However, due to their high cost and complex preparation process, their practical application was limited. Since biomass is available and renewable, the use of agricultural by-products as a carbon source for the preparation of carbon materials from biomass has attracted a great deal of attention from chemists. As a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nano-material, carbon dot has been widely studied due to their simple preparation methods, low cost, and many precursors. Gu et al. (Gu et al., 2022) in situ copolymerized gelatin-based carbon dot (CD) into 2-carboxyethyl (phenyl) phosphoric acid-poly(ethylene terephthalate) (CEPPA-PET), the LOI of CEPPA-gCD-PET was 31 %, but the experimental results had shown that PET cannot reach the V-0 level of vertical combustion only through a single CD.

In this study, we have developed a flame-retardant epoxy resin that utilizes CD as a nano-filler flame retardant synergist to address the aforementioned concerns. This paper aims to address the goal of carbon neutrality and effectively improve the utilization rate of corn straw by preparing a flame retardant synergist based on corn stalk carbon point using corn stalk as raw material. N-doped carbon dot (CD) was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method, and then further used to create a flame retardant epoxy resin composite called EP/ADP/CD. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis confirmed the presence of phosphomolybdate, -OH, and -NH2 on the surface of the CD. Cone calorimetry analysis demonstrated that the production of pHRR, THR, CO2, and CO decreased with the addition of CD. The aim of this study was to design an epoxy resin with high flame retardancy and low toxicity (Zheng et al., 2023) to reduce carbon dioxide emissions and promote the eco-friendly utilization of corn straw.

Bisphenol-A-based epoxy resin (EP, and the epoxy value was 0.51, viscosity: 11000–14000 m·Pa·s) was supplied by Xingchen Synthetic Material Co, Ltd, (Nantong, China). 4,4′-diaminodiphenylmethane (DDM) was obtained from Aladdin Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Corn straw was purchased from Lianyungang, Jiangsu Province. Aluminum diethylhypophosphite (ADP) was purchased from Fuslin Chemical Technology Co Ltd, (Qingdao, China). Phosphomolybdic acid hydrate (PMA) was purchased from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA) was supplied by Damao Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China)

The CD was prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis. First, the beaker was filled with 1.2 g of corn stover, 8 ml of ethylenediamine, 0.4 g of phosphomolybdic acid, and 40 ml of deionized water. After that, the mixture was put in an oven to react for ten hours at 180 °C inside a 100 ml reaction kettle lined with polytetrafluoroethylene. Following the completion of the reaction, the mixture was permitted to naturally cool to room temperature and then separated in a centrifuge set at 8000 rpm/min for 10 minutes in order to remove large particles. The resulting brown supernatant was collected and filtered using a 0.22 μm cellulose ester filter membrane. The filtrate was then dialyzed in a 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 hours to obtain a purified carbon dot solution. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried for 4 days to obtain brown CD powder.

To prepare the EP flame-retardant composites, ADP and CD were dispersed in acetone and then ultrasonicated for 15 minutes. Further, the dispersion was slowly stirred at 60 °C for 30 min until most of the solvent was removed to obtain ADP/CD. ADP/CD was added to the epoxy resin and thoroughly stirred at 80 °C, and then DDM containing the molten state was added to the above EP/ADP/CD mixture under stirring. The mixture was transferred to a vacuum oven and left at 25 bar for 15 minutes to eliminate air bubbles. Finally, the mixture was poured into the mold and pre-cured in an oven at 100°C for 2 h and at 150°C for 2 h.

The total addition amount of the fixed flame retardant was 4 wt%, and CD and ADP were added to EP in different mass ratios, which were recorded as EP/ADP/CD. Pure epoxy resin cured samples without flame retardant were recorded as EP, and cured material samples with only ADP were recorded as EP/ADP. The material composition of the samples can be found in Table 1.
The diluted CD solution was dripped onto an ultra-thin copper mesh and dried. The microstructure of the CD was analyzed by JEM-2100 (Japan).
Functional groups on the surface of carbon dot was performed on a PerkinElmer spectrometer (Massachusetts, USA) in the wavenumber range from 4000 to 650 cm−1 and scanned 64 times.
Content of P and Mo elements doped on the surface of carbon dot were measured by using ICP-OES (PerkinElmer, America) instrument.
The crystal form of carbon dot was measured on a Rigaku D/Max-2200 rotating anode X-ray diffractometer (Almelo, Netherlands) with a scanning rate of 5°/min and a scanning range from 5° to 80°.
The surface structure, element composition and content of carbon dot were observed by a thermospectrometer (Waltham, USA).

The initial decomposition temperature and residual char of the flame retardant samples were measured by the PerkinElmer Pyris 1 Thermal Analyser (Massachusetts, USA). The sample was heated at a rate of 10℃/min from 50℃ to 800℃ under N2 atmosphere.
UL 94 tests of epoxy composites were carried out on a JF-3 oxygen index meter (Nanjing, China). The size of the sample was 130 mm×13 mm×3.2 mm.
LOI tests of epoxy composites were carried out on a JF-3 oxygen index meter (Nanjing, China). The size of sample was 130 mm×6.5 mm×3.2 mm.

The combustion behavior of epoxy composites was characterized on a Cone Calorimeter (Cone) tests by 6810-type Fire Testing Technology CONE (Suzhou, China) according to ISO5660. The size of sample was 100 mm×100 mm×3 mm.

The mechanical properties of epoxy composites were tested by DA 850 (America). The size of the sample was 30 mm×10 mm×3 mm. The temperature range under test was ambient to 250 °C with a heat rate of 10 °C per minute.

The residual char after cone calorimeter test was collected by JEM-7500 F, and the accelerating voltage was 20 kV. The microstructure and surface elemental distribution of residual char were observed by EDS. All the samples were coated with gold before testing.

The gas phase products produced by the decomposition of epoxy composites at high temperature were heated at a rate of 20 °C per minute in an N2 atmosphere by Thermogravimetric Analyzer TGA 8000 (PerkinElmer, America). The temperature range covered by the products was 50–800 °C.

The microstructure of the CD was analyzed by the TEM, and the particle size of the CD was about 3.38 nm. In addition, the HR-TEM image in
Fig. 1 a showed that the CD structure was somewhat crystalline and had a lattice spacing of about 0.26 nm, which suggested that the CD had carbon cores consisting of graphitic carbon and amorphous carbon. The XRD spectrum of CD in Fig. 1 b showed a broad diffraction peak near 2Ɵ=22.36°, which corresponded to the (002) diffraction spectrum of graphitic carbon, which suggested that the structure of CD was a disordered amorphous structure (Zhou et al., 2022).

The absorption spectrum of CD was displayed in Fig. 1 f, with a significant peak at around 250 nm due to the π-π* transition of the SP2 structure. Fig. 1 e demonstrated that CD exhibited a typical excitation dependence when the excitation wavelength was increased from 360 nm to 410 nm. It meant that the emission wavelength changed with the excitation wavelength, and the fluorescence intensity initially increased before decreasing with the increase of emission wavelength. The CD exhibited maximum fluorescence intensity when excited at 400 nm and emitted at 481 nm.

To identify the surface functional groups of the CD, FT-IR ( Fig. 3 a) spectra were performed. Four distinctive absorption peaks were observed at 1060, 986, 892, and 798 cm−1. These peaks were attributed to the stretching vibrational absorption peaks of the Keggin-type octahedral coordination structure of the HPMo anion. In particular, the peaks matched the stretching vibration absorption peaks for (P-Oa), (MoOd), (Mo-Ob-Mo), and (Mo-Oc-Mo). Oa, Od, Ob, and Oc represented the internal, terminal, and bridging oxygens in the Keggin structure, respectively. These four peaks indicated that the prepared CD had a good Keggin structure. Additionally, the stretching vibration absorption peaks of O-H and N-H at 3310 cm−1 and the bending vibration peaks of H-O-H of the water molecule at 1660 cm−1 were shown. Therefore, the FTIR results confirmed the successful grafting of -OH, -NH2, and phosphomolybdate on the CD surface.

The amount of Mo in the CD was determined to be 3.82 % and the amount of P was found to be 1.44 % using ICP-OES. Fig. 3 showed five distinct peaks at binding energies of 284.8, 401, and 533, 133, and 232 eV for C1s, N1s, O1s, P2P, and Mo3d, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 3 c) had three distinct bands at around 284.6 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.5 eV, which can be attributed to the CO bond, the C-C/CC group, and the C-O/C-N groups. For the N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 d), two peaks were observed at 399.8 and 401.8 eV, corresponding to nitrogen oxides and pyridine nitrogen (Yu et al., 2021), confirming the presence of the -NH2 structure. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 3 e) showed two peaks at 532.88 and 531.4 eV from CO and C-O-C/C-OH, consistent with Cls spectra. The Mo3d XPS spectra (Fig. 3 f) had two main peaks located at 235.5, 232.6, and 229.8 eV, indicating the presence of Mo8+, Mo6+, and Mo4+ groups, which verified that phosphomolybdate had been successfully grafted onto the CD surface.
The thermal degradability of the composite resin was obtained by thermogravimetric testing and the results were shown in Fig. 4 and Table 2.

Pure EP began to disintegrate at 349.28℃ and reached its maximum decomposition rate at 378.3℃. The Tinitial of EP/4ADP complex was lowered to 334.11°C, suggesting that the presence of ADP aided in the breakdown of EP. The Tinitial of the EP complex was further decreased to 323.52℃ with the presence of ADP/CD, which was 7.3 % lower compared to pure EP, indicating that ADP and CD may work in concert to promote EP degradation. Due to the abundant carbon content of CD, the degradation rate of flame retardants and epoxy resins was inhibited and the production of char layers was facilitated. Therefore, the heat weight loss rate of EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD at the Tmax stage was significantly smaller than that of pure EP and EP/4ADP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flame retardant performance of EP and EP composites
ce:section.ce:para: The flame retardancy of EP and EP composites was tested using UL-94 and LOI methods. The digital photos and specific values of vertical combustionwere were shown in Fig. 5 and Table 3, and the result of limit oxygen index was displayed in Fig. 6.

Pure EP was flammable with an LOI of 26.5 % and did not have a rating in the UL-94 system. However, when 4 wt% ADP was added, the LOI increased significantly and it self-extinguished after 6.4 seconds. Additionally, when 3.5 % ADP/0.5 % CD was combined with EP, it self-extinguished after 3.2 seconds and received a UL-94 V-0 rating. The LOI of EP composites ranged from 33.8 % to 37.5 %, depending on the mass ratios of ADP and CD in the composites. The study concluded that a specific mass ratio of ADP/CD had a better synergistic flame retardant effect on EP.
EP and EP matrix were evaluated for their flame-retardant performance using the cone calorimetric test. The results were displayed in Fig. 7. A summary of the relevant data was provided in Table 4.

Table 4 revealed that the ignition time for pure epoxy resin is 59 seconds. However, EP/ADP and EP/ADP/CD had ignition times of 47 seconds and 38 seconds, respectively. In comparison to pure epoxy resin, the addition of flame retardant to EP composites caused slightly earlier ignition times, resulting in the initial stage decomposition of EP.

The pHRR of pure EP was measured at 1604 KW/m2. The pHRR of EP was lowered only by the addition of ADP. However, when EP was combined with ADP/CD in the ratio of EP/3.5ADP/0.5 % CD, the pHRR was further reduced. The pHRR of EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD was measured at 1184 KW/m2, which is 51.5 % less than the pHRR of EP alone. The introduction of ADP/CD caused the peak heat release rate of epoxy combustion to occur earlier. This was because the decomposition products of ADP/CD catalyzed the early decomposition of EP into char. The char layer served as a barrier to reduce the intensity of burning in the epoxy resin. An impressive 39.1 % reduction in the THR of EP was observed when ADP and CD were combined, compared to using only ADP. During the combustion of polymer materials, CO and CO2 were the primary harmful gases produced, which caused asphyxiation and fatalities in fires. The increased production of CO and CO2 during combustion elevates the risk factor for fire rescue missions. Therefore, measuring the yield of CO and CO2 was a crucial parameter for evaluating fire hazards. Combining with the graph and table, it can be found that pure EP exhibited a lower COP (0.0388 g/s) and a higher CO2P (0.67 g/s), which indicated that pure EP was fully combusted under the condition of sufficient O2 to produce more CO2 and less CO, while the COP of EP/4ADP increased by 24.2 % and the CO2P decreased by 36.8 % relative to that of pure EP. This is due to the strong dehydrating effect of the thermal decomposition products of ADP, which charred the surface of the covered epoxy resin and produced an expanding char layer. The expanding char layer covered the surface of the epoxy resin during combustion, preventing it from coming into contact with oxygen and leading to incomplete combustion, which produced more CO and less CO2. Whereas, EP/3.5ADP/0.5 %CD exhibited less COP (0.0312 g/s) and CO2P (0.198 g/s). Compared with the pure epoxy resin, the COP of EP/3.5ADP/0.5 %CD was reduced by 19.6 % and CO2P was reduced by 70.4 %, which indicated that the ADP/CD facilitated the generation of a denser expanded carbon layer, inhibited the release of combustion-generated CO and CO2, and demonstrated a positive effect of toxicity reduction.

EHC was a measure of how well volatile gases are burning in a gas phase flame. It was calculated by dividing the measured rate of heat release by the rate of mass loss at a specific moment in time. The lower level of EHC, the more non-combustible gases were present in the gas phase. The av-EHC of pure EP was 33.09 MJ/kg, and that of EP/4ADP, EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD were 27.69 MJ/kg and 18.2 MJ/kg, suggesting further that the compound flame retardants have more noncombustible gases in the gas phase, and the both of them showed excellent synergistic effect in the gas phase flame retardant mechanism.

The Fire Performance Index (FPI) was a measure of the potential fire hazard of a material based on two factors: the time it takes to ignite (TTI) and the peak heat release rate (pHRR). A higher FPI value meant that the material posed less danger in a fire. The Fire Growth Index (FGI) measured how quickly a fire spreads after the material ignites. A high FGI value indicated a shorter time to reach a higher heat release rate and a longer time to reach the peak of the heat release rate. Materials with high FGI and low FGI demonstrate a higher level of safety overall. The addition of CD significantly reduced the FGI and increased the FPI values of EP, suggesting that CD can improve the fire safety of EP.
(1) FPI = TTI / pHRR
(2)
FGI = pHRR / Time to reach pHRR

The flame retardant characteristics of previously published EP composites from recent years were compared with the findings of our work in Fig. 8 . The findings of this investigation were at the forefront in terms of minimal loading of flame retardants, a decrease of CO2, and pHRR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DMA analysis of EP and EP composites
ce:section.ce:para: DMA is an essential technique for testing the mechanical and viscoelastic properties of materials at programmed controlled temperatures. The storage modulus, loss modulus, and tanδ of pure EP, EP/4ADP, and EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD composites under vibratory loading as a function of temperature were shown in Fig. 9. The storage modulus of all samples progressively dropped as the temperature rose from room temperature to 190°C. The addition of ADP to EP caused a decrease in storage modulus from 1509 to 1204 MPa due to enhanced internal depletion of the epoxy chain segments induced by ADP. However, the storage modulus of the composite increased dramatically rose to 1490 MPa after the addition of CD to EP/ADP, which was attributed to the homogeneously dispersed CD enhanced the cross-linking density of the polymer. As a result, the polymer became more rigid and the mobility of the molecular chains was restricted.

In general, conventional flame retardant methods greatly reduce mechanical properties due to interfacial interactions and inhomogeneous dispersion. The homogenous texture of the pure EP fracture surface, which exhibits the usual features of brittle fracture, may be shown in Fig. 10 a. In contrast, when the flame retardants ADP/CD was added, there was no agglomeration in the substrate. Meanwhile, some folds appeared on the fracture surface (Fig. 10 b), indicating that the fracture of EP shifted from a brittle fracture to a rigid fracture. Based on Fig. 10 c, it was observed that the tensile and impact strengths of EP/ADP were significantly lower in comparison to pure EP. Whereas, the tensile modulus of EP composites increased marginally as a result of the addition of CD, which was measured to be 46.1 MPa. Despite this increase, the tensile modulus was still less than that of pure EP. The creation of metal complexes between ADP and CD enhanced the cross-linking strength of EP and had good compatibility with the EP matrix, resulting in a 19.9 % increase in the impact strength of EP/ADP/CD compared to pure EP. Additionally, the impact strength of epoxy can be strengthened by the inclusion of CD, which can more effectively block the erratic movement of molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flame-retardant mechanism of EP and EP composites

ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the flame retardant mechanism of ADP/CD in the condensed phase relative to EP, the morphology ( Fig. 11) of the residual char after burning of the Cone was recognized by using a digital camera and SEM. It was observed that there was just a tiny bit of residual char remaining and the pure EP was almost completely burned. At high temperatures, ADP carbonized to produce aluminum phosphonate (AlP) with a high melting point. During the combustion process, the carbonization product can produce a carbon layer that covers the substrate's surface. Therefore, the char layer of EP/4ADP flame retardant curing compounds expanded after burning, but the pores were large and connected. The incorporation of CD nanoparticles with -OH on the surface enabled hydrogen bonding with diethyl hypophosphorous acid, forming a substance that enhanced flame retardancy in the condensed phase and reinforced the char layer. EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD formed a inflated and stable swollen of char layer that was roughly 3.5 cm in height. The dense char layer can prevent the air from coming into contact with the polymer.

Moreover, it will hinder the gas generated by pyrolysis from diffusing into the matrix, so that the polymer can not get adequate oxygen and heat, gradually extinguished, so as to achieve the effect of flame retardant. Elemental Mapping ( Fig. 12) showed that the residual char of EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD contains a P element and a slight amount of Mo element. The P-containing group was decomposed by thermolysis to form P-containing radicals, which reacted with H· and OH· to form phosphoric acid and disperse in the condensed phase, and phosphoric acid was contributed to the rapid carbonization of epoxy resin. Mo-containing groups can be used as catalysts for resin cross-linking to carbon and dehydrogenation reactions (Lou et al., 2023). The introduction of ADP encouraged the formation of char layer. Meanwhile, the early breakdown of EP and the formation of a char layer, which functioned as a surface barrier and lowers surface heat transfer, were both facilitated by the introduction of highly graphitized CD. Therefore, when the epoxy resin was modified with ADP/CD as a flame retardant, the resulting char layer generated during combustion was denser and more continuous, leading to improved flame retardancy of the composite material.

TG-FTIR analysis was performed on the gas generated during the thermal degradation of EP and its composites in order to examine the flame retardant characteristics of ADP/CD in the gas phase. Fig. 13 displays a 3D spectrum of the infrared absorption curves of pure EP and EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD pyrolysis gas products at various temperatures. The pyrolysis products of EP composites resembled those of pure epoxy resin, but the addition of ADP/CD decreased the absorption peak intensity, effectively inhibiting the release of gaseous products during the combustion of epoxy resin.

Fig. 14 showed the FTIR spectra of pure EP and EP substances at different temperatures. Pure EP exhibited peaks at 3656 cm−1, 3100–2800 cm−1, 2360 cm−1, 2190 cm−1, and 1600–1510 cm−1, corresponding to phenol, hydrocarbon, CO2, CO, carbonyl compounds, and aromatic compounds, separately. As the temperature increased, the peak of -OH gradually disappeared. A portion of ADP was able to delocalize alkyl groups (Lou et al., 2023) and then produce PO· and P· radicals. It is possible for these PO· and P· radicals to efficiently absorb the H· and HO· radicals in the gas phase, thus inhibiting the radical chain reaction and playing a flame retardant role. However, EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD showed an obvious absorption peak of N-H (ν=3300–3500 cm−1) after 350℃.

Fig. 15 and Fig. 16 further assessed the impact of ADP/CD on the pyrolysis behavior of EP by comparing the absorption peak intensity and time of each pyrolysis product of EP and its composites. Compared with pure EP, EP/3.5ADP/0.5CD exhibited significantly reduced absorption peak intensity of aromatic compounds, aliphatic hydrocarbon structures, carbonyl compounds and CO. These findings suggested that ADP/CD played a role in hindering combustion chain reactions in the gas phase, thus enhancing the flame retardant properties of EP composites even more.

Based on the explanation above, the flame retardant mechanism of ADP/CD in EP is summarized as follows ( Scheme 1). Being an organophosphorus flame retardant, ADP breaks down upon heating to produce polyphosphoric acid. The matrix is rapidly dehydrated by this acid, making it flame retardant in the condensed phase. The CD with N, P, and Mo elements undergoes thermal decomposition to produce NH3 and N2, which absorb heat and dilute oxygen concentration, serving as a gas phase flame retardant. The CD has a large specific surface area, which is capable of forming an impermeable network structure in the carbon layer, creating a barrier effect and trapping free radicals during combustion. ADP as Phosphorus-containing flame retardants produce free radical inhibitors that hinder the complete combustion of EP in the gas phase, resulting in more non-combustible smoke particles. When gas is released, it can react with the char layer formed by organophosphorus, which causes the surface to expand and creates a more concentrated and uniform char layer. As a result, there is a greater and more effective interaction between the condensed and gas phases.

Here, the biomased CD with N, P, and Mo elements was successfully fabricated via a one-step hydrothermal method. The thermal stability, mechanical properties, and flame retardancy in relation to ADP/CD of EP were conducted. The EP composites (containing 3.5 wt% ADP/0.5 wt% CD) achieved a UL-94 V-0 rating and showed high LOI values. The amount of residual char after the cone test increased with the addition of ADP/CD. Meanwhile, it also exhibited superior fire safety with a noticeable decline in the values of PHRR and THR compared with pure EP. Significantly, the cone test findings demonstrated that adding ADP/CD enhanced flame-retardant qualities and markedly decreased CO and CO2 emissions of EP. This improvement was attributed to the catalytic carbon-forming and radical scavenging ability of ADP/CD and the synergistic effect of ADP/CD in the epoxy resin matrix. In essence, this composite is an epoxy resin with high flame retardancy and low toxicity.

Peixiao Sun: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Yang Leng: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zhao Wang: Writing – review & editing. Miaojun Xu: Writing – review & editing. Bin Li: Supervision. Xiaoli Li: Writing – review & editing. Hongjian Zhang: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52103251) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572022BU06).
